event: Judith Leaving the Tent of Holofernes
symbols: sword in Judith's hand — the instrument of divine deliverance through human courage
severed head of Holofernes — the defeat of the enemy of God's people through an unlikely agent
red tent curtain — the blood-stained domain of the tyrant now conquered
Judith stepping forward — the faithful moving from the place of danger into freedom and victory
description: A full-length figure of a young woman in an elegant dress of golden-ochre and pink strides forward from a red tent. In her right hand she carries a sword pointed downward. Behind her, an older woman in darker robes follows, carrying a large bearded male head. The red tent curtain frames the scene dramatically. Judith's expression is calm and resolute. Her garments flow with the movement of her stride. The composition is vertical and dynamic.
theological_description: The Book of Judith tells of a widow who saved Israel by beheading the Assyrian general Holofernes. When the enemy had besieged Bethulia and Israel faced annihilation, Judith declared: 'The Lord will rescue Israel by my hand' (Judith 8:33). She entered Holofernes' tent, and while he lay drunk, she took his sword and struck off his head (Judith 13:7-8). This act of divine deliverance through an unlikely human instrument follows the biblical pattern: God uses the weak to shame the strong (1 Corinthians 1:27). Judith's courage echoes Jael's killing of Sisera (Judges 4:21) and David's defeat of Goliath (1 Samuel 17:50) — in each case, God's people are delivered by those the world would least expect. Judith's canticle of victory — 'The Lord Almighty has foiled them by the hand of a woman' (Judith 16:5) — anticipates Mary's Magnificat, where God 'has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble' (Luke 1:52).

scripture_refs.reason: Judith beheading Holofernes with his own sword — the narrative depicted
God chose the weak to shame the strong — deliverance through an unlikely heroine
Jael killing Sisera — the pattern of a woman delivering Israel from a military enemy
God brings down the mighty and lifts the humble — Judith's victory anticipates the Magnificat
